label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out this locker problem. So there are 100 lockers, all starting closed. Then there are 100 people, each one toggling (opening or closing) specific lockers. Person 1 opens every locker. Person 2 toggles every second locker, so that would be lockers 2, 4, 6, etc. Then Person 3 toggles every third locker, and so on up to Person 100, who toggles just the 100th locker. The question is, how many lockers are open at the end? Hmm. Let me think about how each locker is toggled. Each locker number, say locker number n, is going to be toggled by every person whose number is a factor of n. For example, locker 6 will be toggled by person 1, 2, 3, and 6. Because 1×6=6, 2×3=6, etc. So the number of times a locker is toggled is equal to the number of its factors. Now, since all lockers start closed, each time they're toggled, they switch state: closed to open, open to closed. So if a locker is toggled an even number of times, it will end up closed again. If it's toggled an odd number of times, it will end up open. Therefore, the lockers that remain open are those with an odd number of factors. Wait, but most numbers have an even number of factors, right? Because factors come in pairs. For example, 6 has factors 1 and 6, 2 and 3. So that's four factors, which is even. Except for perfect squares. Let me recall: perfect squares have an odd number of factors because one of the factors is repeated. Like 16: factors are 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. Here, 4 is paired with itself (4×4=16), so it's only counted once. So numbers that are perfect squares have an odd number of factors. Therefore, the lockers that are open at the end are the ones whose numbers are perfect squares. Because only they have an odd number of factors, leading to an odd number of toggles, which leaves them open. So now, how many perfect squares are there between 1 and 100 inclusive? Well, the squares of 1 through 10, since 10² is 100. Let me check: 1² = 1 2² = 4 3² = 9 4² = 16 5² = 25 6² = 36 7² = 49 8² = 64 9² = 81 10² = 100 Yep, that's 10 numbers. So there are 10 perfect squares between 1 and 100. Therefore, 10 lockers will be open at the end. Let me double-check with a smaller example. Let's say there are 10 lockers. Then the open lockers should be 1, 4, 9. Wait, 9 is within 10. So that's 3 lockers. Let me simulate: Person 1 opens all lockers: 1-10 open. Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker: 2,4,6,8,10. So those become closed. Person 3 toggles every 3rd locker: 3,6,9. So locker 3 was closed (since Person 1 opened, Person 2 didn't touch it? Wait, no: initial state is all closed. Person 1 opens every locker. So after Person 1, all are open. Then Person 2 toggles every 2nd locker, so locker 2 is closed, 4 closed, etc. Then Person 3 toggles lockers 3,6,9. So locker 3 was open, now closed. Locker 6 was closed (toggled by Person 2), now open. Locker 9 was open, now closed. Then Person 4 toggles 4,8. Locker 4 was closed, now open. Locker 8 was closed, now open. Then Person 5 toggles 5,10. Locker 5 was open, now closed. Locker 10 was closed, now open. Person 6 toggles 6. It was open (toggled by Person 3?), let me see: locker 6 was toggled by 1 (open), 2 (closed), 3 (open). Then Person 6 toggles it again (closed). So locker 6 becomes closed. Person 7 toggles 7: open to closed. Person 8 toggles 8: open (from Person 4) to closed. Person 9 toggles 9: closed (from Person 3) to open. Person 10 toggles 10: open (from Person 5) to closed. So the final open lockers would be locker 1 (toggled once by Person 1, then never again?), locker 4 (toggled by 1, 2, 4. Opened, closed, opened again). Locker 9: toggled by 1, 3, 9. Open, closed, open. And locker 16? Well in 10 lockers, there's no 16. So in 10 lockers, lockers 1, 4, 9 are open. Which are the squares of 1, 2, 3. Since 3²=9
